data.title: Treasury's Bessent Sends A Clear Message To The Fed On Rates
data.description:
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is signaling a far more cautious tone on interest rates.

Speaking at an event in Washington, Bessent said the Federal Reserve should take a wait and see approach before cutting rates, especially with the ongoing conflict in Iran adding another layer of uncertainty to the outlook.

It's not that he's against rate cuts altogether. In fact, he said they will likely come at some point. But in the near term, the focus should be on watching how things unfold both geopolitically and economically.

Part of that view comes from the strength the economy showed earlier this year. According to Bessent, the U.S. entered 2026 on a solid footing, giving the Fed some room to be patient rather than rush into policy changes.

There's also the inflation angle. While oil and gas prices have been moving higher due to the conflict, Bessent doesn't see those increases becoming a long-term problem or feeding into broader inflation expectations.Hence, it's clear that the path to rate cuts may take longer than hoped, as the Fed stays cautious in an increasingly uncertain environment.

data.title: Cineflicks Launches First Presale Round as It Develops Participation-Based Streaming Platform
data.description:
The Cineflicks platform is designed to offer users access to movies, shows, and digital content while introducing a structured engagement system within the platform. The model focuses on integrating content consumption with user participation, aiming to create a more interactive entertainment experience.

According to the team, Cineflicks is currently in the beta stage of development. Core features under development include a streaming interface, user profiles, content discovery systems, and reward tracking mechanisms. The platform also includes Web3 wallet integration to support digital asset management and transparent tracking of user activity within the ecosystem.

The announcement comes at a time when global streaming consumption continues to grow, with audiences spending increasing amounts of time on digital entertainment platforms. While streaming services have expanded significantly over the past decade, most platforms continue to operate on a passive consumption model where users access content without participating in the broader platform ecosystem.

Cineflicks is positioning its platform around a model that introduces participation-based engagement, where user activity is integrated into the overall platform experience. The company states that this approach is intended to align user engagement with platform growth while maintaining a familiar streaming interface.

The first presale round marks an early phase of ecosystem participation, with additional stages expected as the platform progresses toward its public rollout. The presale is structured in multiple phases, supporting the gradual expansion of the Cineflicks ecosystem.

The company plans to continue development of the platform alongside expanding its content library, partnerships, and user base in the coming months.

About Cineflicks

Cineflicks is an upcoming digital entertainment platform focused on streaming movies, shows, and web-based content. The platform is being developed to combine content access with user participation, supported by a structured digital ecosystem.

Media Contact

Email: marketing@cineflicks.io 

Website: https://cineflicks.io 

Twitter: https://x.com/cineflicksott 

Instagram: https://instagram.com/cineflicksott

data.title: Stocks Are Back to Pre-Iran War Levels. Earnings Will Dictate Where They Go Next. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Martin Baccardax

Global investors are growing cautious on stocks, and the hit the world economy is likely to take from the U.S. war with Iran, but not to the point where they're ready to bet against the market, which is near all-time highs heading into the start of second quarter.

Bank of America's benchmark Fund Manager Survey, published monthly, suggests the world's biggest investors are expecting the fastest inflation pressures in five years, the slowest GDP growth in four and elevated oil prices for the remainder of 2026.

However, while the survey indicates the largest bearish tilt since last June, investors are still overweight stocks, aren't moving into cash and don't see recession on the horizon.

That likely means investors need both Federal Reserve interest rate cuts and better than expected earnings growth to sustain the market's sudden cease-fire optimism.

The S&P 500, in fact, has both reclaimed its prewar levels and nudged into positive territory for the year this week, thanks in part to reports suggesting a willingness in both Washington and Tehran to pursue diplomacy over renewed attacks in the continuing conflict.

The benchmark is also just 20 points from the all-time peak it reached in late January.

"Investors believe that the war will end soon, although whether that means in a couple of days, weeks or months isn't yet clear," said David Morrison, senior market analyst at Trade Nation. "What is clear is that the markets don't believe it will take years."

"Nobody wants to be under-exposed to risk assets, let alone be short, should the war suddenly conclude, or even if the Strait of Hormuz was unblocked," he added.

A solid start to the first quarter earnings season, including Street-beating updates from JPMorgan, Citigroup, and Johnson & Johnson on Tuesday, is also providing support.

What could also prove crucial to the market's near-term performance is the level at which companies surpass Wall Street's earnings forecasts. Data from FactSet suggests collective S&P 500 profits are likely to rise 12.2% from last year, but if the historic "beat rate" is extended into the mix, that figure could rise to around 19%, which would take the overall tally to around $632 billion.

"As corporate earnings are the biggest driver of stock returns, this level of steadfast earnings growth is an incredibly positive sign given that the market has been hammered by the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz," said Fundstrat's economic strategist Hardika Singh.

A muted reading of producer price inflation pressures over the month of March, which included the impact of the surge in global crude prices, might have also supported bets on a Fed rate cut between now and the end of the year.

"After a strong couple of months, the downside surprise in core producer prices in March offers some encouragement for the Fed that pipeline inflation pressures aren't spiraling out of control," said Stephen Brown, chief North America economist at Capital Economics.

Still, investors will probably have to wait a bit longer for the Fed to react, as the CME Group's FedWatch tool doesn't signal the odds of a quarter point reduction in the central bank's key borrowing rate until the summer of 2027.

But even that might not stop the S&P 500 from finishing out the first half of the year, and the whole of 2026, with solid double-digit gains.

Lori Calvasina, head of U.S. equity strategy at RBC Capital Markets, is holding on to her end of year price target of 7750 points, a 12% advance from current levels.

But she's not factoring in Fed cuts, sees inflation holding north of 3% for much of the year, and expects benchmark 10-year Treasury note yields to trend toward the 4.5% level. All of those moves would normally signal downside pressure on stocks, but Calvasina has a theory as to why that's not the case.

"Overall, the story that our models tell is that the S&P 500 can stay on a path headed to 7,750 over the course of the next year, supported by a recovery in investor sentiment from deeply bearish levels and a solid earnings growth and economic backdrop that don't incur too much damage (as a whole) from recent disruption to energy markets and the Middle East," she said.

Write to Martin Baccardax at martin.baccardax@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson Financial Chief Sees 'Clear Line of Sight' to Double-Digit Growth by 2030 — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

Johnson & Johnson has reinvented itself several times in recent years. In 2021, it was best known as the developer of a Covid vaccine. In 2023, it spun off its consumer health division to focus on pharmaceuticals and medical devices. Its latest earnings report signals the company has reinvented itself again.

J&J posted adjusted earnings of $2.70 a share for the first quarter, narrowly beating out the $2.68 a share analysts had anticipated. Sales of $24.06 billion outstripped Wall Street's calls for $23.61 billion and marked a 10% increase from the same period last year.

Sales grew by more than 11% in J&J's Innovative Medicine arm, which includes its oncology portfolio, and 7.7% in its medical technology division. On an operational basis, the metric increased 7.4% and 4.6%, respectively. Medical device sales came to $8.6 billion, in line with estimates.

Demand for J&J's cancer treatments were one driver. J&J credited the quarter's strong performance to sales of Darzalex, Carvykti, and two other prescription medicines within oncology. Other standouts included its anti-inflammatory drug Tremfya and nasal spray Spravato, which is approved for use in patients with treatment-resistant depression.

"The fact the performance came from key brands that are already on the market suggests this is de-risked," Chief Financial Officer Joe Wolk said in an interview with Barron's. "We've added new products to our portfolio, and it gives us a clear line of sight to double-digit growth by the end of the decade. When you think this is going to be a hundred-billion-dollar business by the end of the year, that's saying something."

J&J is targeting full-year sales of between $100.3 billion and $101.3 billion in 2026, compared with earlier calls for $100 billion to $101 billion. The company sees adjusted earnings of $11.45 to $11.65 a share in 2026, up slightly from a prior range of $11.43 to $11.63 a share. Analysts were looking for $100.6 billion in sales and profit of $11.54 a share, slightly below the midpoint of both ranges.

Shares reversed earlier losses and rose 1.2% on Tuesday. The S&P 500 was up 1.1%. The stock has handily outperformed the broader market in 2026: Through Monday's close, J&J has risen 15% for the year. The benchmark index eked out a 0.6% gain over the same period.

Sales of Stelara, formerly one of J&J's biggest moneymakers, cratered 60% in the quarter. The drug lost major patent protection in late 2023, but J&J struck agreements with manufacturers to delay the market entry of competing biosimilars until 2025. Sales have fallen steadily since then.

Other products made up for lost ground. Tremfya, which is positioned by J&J as the successor to Stelara, raked in $1.6 billion globally, up from $956 million in 2025 and better than consensus estimates. Darzalex, the company's best selling product, brought in nearly $4 billion, up from $3.2 billion last year and a hair above Wall Street's forecast of $3.9 billion.

The narrative around other products is still taking shape. Just last month, regulators approved J&J's Icotyde as the first targeted oral peptide for plaque psoriasis. Wolk indicated the company is excited about its prospects.

"It has equivalent efficacy and as clean a safety profile as biologics," the financial chief said, nodding to patients' general aversion to the medication class, which is caused by anything from a fear of needles to the perception of biologics as a last resort.

"We think that opens up a new market opportunity, and some of the investment you saw in the quarter is going towards the success of that," Wolk added. Research and development expenses increased 9.4% in the latest quarter.

While J&J is dipping into new markets, the company's oncology d ivision continues to be a focal point, accounting for the bulk of total sales in 2025. The drugmaker boasts three treatments for multiple myeloma, which it sells under the brand names Darzalex, Tecvayli, and Carvykti.

Taken together, these products are expected to drive strength in J&J's cancer segment, where management has guided to $50 billion in sales by 2030 — an "ambitious but achievable" goal, TD Cowen analysts remarked in a research note last week.

With Tuesday's guidance raise, J&J has signaled its commitment to achieving double-digit growth by the end of the decade. The company anticipates sales will rise about 7% this year, up from an earlier projection for 6.7% growth.

Darzalex faces its own patent cliff starting in the mid-2030s. However, the company is already bolstering other parts of its portfolio. Along with Bristol Myers Squibb, J&J appears to have cornered the market for multiple myeloma — its CAR-T cell therapy, Carvykti, achieved blockbuster status for the first time in 2025.

As for other business lines, J&J has plans for its burgeoning medical technology segment. The company is developing a digital robot that puts it head-to-head with Intuitive Surgical, and expects regulatory approval for that product by the end of the year. J&J also faces little competition in the market for psychiatric drugs, where analysts have flagged attractive growth opportunities.

Regulators in November approved the use of Caplyta, an antipsychotic drug acquired through J&J's buyout of Intra-Cellular Therapies, as an adjunctive therapy with antidepressants.

It remains to be seen whether Caplyta will emerge as one of J&J's new blockbusters, as the company's cancer medicines continue to dominate its Innovative Medicine segment. The acquisition was a slight drag on segment sales in the latest quarter.

The drugmaker separately announced Tuesday that its board had approved a 3.1% dividend hike to $1.34 a share from $1.30, representing the 64th consecutive year J&J has increased its payout.

Analysts may believe J&J is positioned for growth, but there are some drags on the stock. Johnson & Johnson faces tens of thousands of lawsuits predicated on claims the company knowingly sold talcum baby powder contaminated with cancer-causing asbestos.

J&J repeatedly sought to protect its assets by transferring the legal liability to a subsidiary and then having that unit file for bankruptcy protection. Its latest attempt concerned a subsidiary called Red River Talc, but that effort was struck down in Texas bankruptcy court, paving the way for individual lawsuits to resume.

At of the end of the fourth quarter, the company faced 74,360 talc-related lawsuits, according to the latest annual filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission. Johnson & Johnson maintains its baby powder, now sold under Kenvue, is safe and asbestos-free.

Even in the face of these challenges, the stock has remained in analysts' good graces. Of 29 firms tracked by FactSet, 17 rate it at Buy or the equivalent. Just two rate it at Sell.

"We think there's a lot more room to run with the stock price," Wolk told Barron's. "If you parlay conviction, certainty, with a higher growth profile, that should be very attractive to investors as we move forward."

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Google's Latest Gemini Upgrade Signals A Bigger Robotics Push
data.description:
Google is pushing further into robotics and is making its latest AI tools available for other businesses to build on.

DeepMind has introduced an upgraded version of its Gemini robotics model, Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6, and made it available to developers via its API and AI Studio. The focus is on making robots smarter at understanding and interacting with the real world.

This new version is designed to improve spatial awareness, object detection, and movement tracking. Put simply, the goal is to help machines see and interpret their surroundings more accurately, which is a big step toward more autonomous behavior.One interesting piece is how it handles tasks. The system can recognize when something hasn't worked and decide whether to try again or move on.

Additionally, Google is also leaning into collaboration here, inviting developers to test the model and flag where it falls short so it can improve faster.For investors, this isn't about immediate revenue. It's about where AI is heading next, and robotics is clearly becoming a bigger part of that conversation.

data.title: Canada Finance Minister to Present Spring Economic Update on April 28 — Update
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canada intends to present an economic update on April 28 that will shed light on the impact of higher energy prices on the government's bottom line.

Finance Minister François-Philippe Champagne said Tuesday the update would reinforce government efforts to rebuild an economy that became too reliant on trade with the U.S. to spark growth.

Canada's Liberal government changed last year the timing of the government's budgeting process. Under the new system, officials now issue an annual budget plan in the fall, to be followed by an update in the spring. Previously, it was the reverse, with an update in the fall and the budget in the spring.

Earlier Tuesday, Canada said it would provide a temporary fuel-tax cut--starting next week until Labor Day-- to help households cope with the financial strain from higher energy prices. Gas prices have climbed more than 30% due to conflict in the Middle East, pitting the U.S. versus Iran, which has largely stalled oil-tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz.

Canada is a net exporter of crude oil, so national income stands to increase from higher energy prices. Economists at BMO Capital Markets estimate a 40% rise in oil prices for a full year could boost government revenue by nearly 5 billion Canadian dollars, or the equivalent of US$3.62 billion.

BMO economists expect the overall impact on federal finances from higher energy prices this time to be neutral, as higher revenue would be offset by slower economic activity and a rise in borrowing costs. Their estimate was provided prior to the Liberal government's plan for fuel-tax relief.

The Bank of Canada, in its most recent economic outlook, anticipated 1.1% growth this year. As it happens, the central bank issues its next rate-policy decision the day after the spring economic update, on April 29.

The International Monetary Fund on Tuesday said it expects Canada to record 1.5% growth in 2026.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: Nvidia Expands AI Push With Quantum Models
data.description:
NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA, Financials) is expanding its artificial intelligence portfolio with the launch of a new family of open-source models designed for quantum computing.

The new "Ising" models use AI to handle complicated operations in order to make quantum systems more useful. One model is all about calibration. It lets systems read data from quantum processors and change how they work in real time. Another one is meant to fix mistakes, which helps make quantum computing more accurate and stable.

The advancement is in line with Nvidia's larger plan to make AI a basic part of all new technologies. The startup wants to solve some of the main problems with scaling quantum systems by adding AI to quantum computing workflows.

Companies, research organizations, and businesses like IonQ, Inc. are already using the models. This shows that there is early interest in merging AI with quantum development.

Nvidia thinks that the quantum computing market will develop a lot over the next ten years, opening up new options for the company beyond its main GPU business. For investors, the move means that the business will keep growing into other high-growth areas as it creates its long-term AI ecosystem.

data.title: Goldman Sachs to use options strategy for planned Bitcoin income ETF
data.description:
Goldman Sachs has filed with the US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) to launch a Bitcoin-linked exchange-traded fund designed to generate income while limiting exposure to the cryptocurrency’s volatility, according to a preliminary prospectus dated April 14.

The proposed Goldman Sachs Bitcoin Premium Income ETF would aim to deliver current income alongside capital appreciation by investing primarily in spot Bitcoin exchange-traded products (ETPs) and related options, rather than holding Bitcoin (BTC) directly.

The fund would generate yield by selling call options on Bitcoin-linked ETPs, a strategy that can produce premium income but may cap upside in rising markets.

According to the filing, the actively managed fund would maintain at least 80% exposure to Bitcoin-linked assets and could allocate as much as 25% of its holdings through a Cayman Islands subsidiary, a structure commonly used to gain commodities exposure under the US Investment Company Act.

The fund expects to vary its options “overwrite” strategy — that is, selling call options against its holdings — between roughly 40% and 100% of its Bitcoin exposure depending on market conditions, and may distribute a significant portion of returns as income or return of capital.

It would gain exposure through a mix of spot Bitcoin ETPs and derivatives, combining direct holdings with options-based positions. The strategy may perform better in flat or moderately rising markets but could underperform during strong rallies as upside is capped.

Eric Balchunas, ETF analyst at Bloomberg, described the product as “Boomer Candy” in a post on X, suggesting the structure may appeal to investors seeking income and lower volatility over full upside exposure.

Separately, Goldman Chair and CEO David Solomon told analysts on Monday that the company last week closed on its acquisition of Innovator Capital Management, an issuer of defined outcome exchange-traded funds. The addition of Innovator’s 170 ETFs puts Goldman in the top 10 of global active ETF providers, Solomon said on the first-quarter earnings call.

Active crypto ETFs gain traction as strategies evolve beyond price tracking

The filing from Goldman Sachs comes as asset managers move beyond basic price-tracking crypto funds, with more complex and actively managed strategies gaining traction across the ETF market.

In January, Bitwise Asset Management launched an actively managed ETF designed to hedge against currency debasement. The fund allocates across assets including Bitcoin, precious metals and mining equities, reflecting a broader push to integrate digital assets into diversified, macro-focused portfolios.

In March, T. Rowe Price amended its filing with the SEC for a proposed actively managed crypto ETF that would invest directly in digital assets. The updated prospectus outlines a portfolio that may include assets such as Bitcoin, Ethereum (ETH) and Solana (SOL).

Fund issuer 21Shares is also expanding into more sophisticated strategies. In February, the company launched a Europe-listed ETP tied to Strategy’s preferred stock (STRC), offering exposure to a yield-generating instrument linked to the company’s Bitcoin-focused capital strategy.

Speaking to Cointelegraph, 21Shares President Duncan Moir said the shift reflects broader demand for more advanced products, noting that crypto is “particularly well-suited to active management.”

According to a March report compiled by Morningstar and Goldman Sachs Asset Management, active ETFs held nearly $1.8 trillion in assets globally at the end of 2025, with flows significantly outpacing passive products.

data.title: Intel Gains on Strong CPU Demand Outlook
data.description:
Intel Corporation (INTC, Financials) shares moved higher in premarket trading after Susquehanna raised its price target on the company, pointing to continued strength in demand for its processors.

The company raised its aim from $65 to $80 because it said there was "insatiable" demand for CPUs, especially in the server market. The analyst thinks that Intel will do about the same as or a little better than expected in the first quarter, thanks to higher performance in data center chips.

The improvement adds to the rising hope that Intel will do well in the AI-driven computing cycle. As the workloads of artificial intelligence grow, the need for server infrastructure, including CPUs, grows as well. This makes Intel even more important in the market.

The call also comes at a time when people are feeling differently about semiconductor businesses that make AI infrastructure. Most of the attention has been on GPUs, but CPUs are becoming more and more vital for handling and coordinating complicated workloads.

The increase in the price target suggests that investors believe Intel will be able to fulfill demand and deliver on time. People will now be looking at the company's upcoming financial reports to see if it can keep up the good work and turn high demand into more money.

data.title: The Physical Oil Market Isn't Buying This Price Pullback Yet — WSJ
data.description:
By David Uberti

A sharp selloff Tuesday dragged oil futures to their lowest prices in weeks, even as refiners continued scrambling for real-world cargoes of crude.

  • Benchmark U.S. crude futures slid 7.9%, to $91.28 a barrel, alongside renewed hopes for U.S.-Iran peace talks. It was the lowest settlment value since March 25. Brent futures fell 4.6% to $94.79 a barrel.
  • A spot-pricing benchmark for the Brent market also fell Tuesday, according to Argus Media, but those supplies still traded at nearly $125 a barrel. Near-term U.S. oil shipments to Europe tracked by Argus ran more than $20 higher.

The divergence between physical and financial prices has scrambled the outlook for the Iran war's impact and pressured fuelmakers trying to shield themselves from big market swings.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Blink Charging Teams with The Rosemyr Corporation to Celebrate Earth Day at Morganton Plaza in North Carolina
data.description:
Blink Offering Two Hours of Free EV Charging at Morganton Plaza and Week-Long Charging Credit Promotion starting Earth Day (April 22, 2026)

Bowie, MD, April 14, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Blink Charging Co.  (“Blink” or the “Company”), a leading global owner, operator, and provider of electric vehicle (EV) charging equipment and services, is teaming with The Rosemyr Corporation to celebrate Earth Day with the offer of two hours of free charging at The Rosemyr Corporation’s Morganton Plaza shopping center in Morganton, North Carolina. Additionally, EV drivers can receive a $5 charging credit from April 22-29 for submitting a review of the site on Plugshare.

Blink Charging recently installed a new high-powered Blink-owned and operated DC fast charging site in the Morganton Plaza parking lot. The site offers four DC fast charger plugs and two different connector options to support fast and convenient charging.

To celebrate Earth Day and the new chargers’ installation, Blink is offering EV drivers the opportunity to enjoy free charging for two hours on Earth Day (April 22 from 12pm to 2pm ET) at the Morganton Plaza parking lot at 112 East Fleming Drive, Morganton, NC 28655.

“We’re excited to support The Rosemyr Corporation and Morganton Plaza and provide solutions for the western North Carolina region’s EV charging infrastructure needs,” said Jen Yokely, Chief Marketing Officer at Blink. “This collaborative effort with our fellow supporters of electrification demonstrates the type of high-powered fast charging sites that support predictable dwell times and represent compelling long-term growth and value creation opportunities. This Earth Day promotion will further draw visibility to the innovative chargers now ready for EV drivers in the area and customers of the shopping center.”

“By teaming with Blink and offering EV charging to the local community and guests of Morganton Plaza, we reaffirm our shared mission to bring a heightened level of convenience to the people of Morganton, and surrounding residents,” said Michelle O’Keefe of The Rosemyr Corporation. “We look forward to working closely with Blink to make our sustainability and accessibility goals a reality – not just for Earth Day, but for every day moving forward.”

###

About Blink Charging

Blink Charging Co. is a global leader in electric vehicle (EV) charging equipment and services, enabling drivers, hosts, and fleets to easily transition to electric transportation through innovative charging solutions. Blink’s principal line of products and services include Blink’s EV charging network (“Blink Network”), EV charging equipment, and EV charging services. The Blink Network uses proprietary, cloud-based software that operates, maintains, and tracks the EV charging stations connected to the network and the associated charging data. Blink has established key strategic partnerships for rolling out adoption across numerous location types, including parking facilities, multifamily residences and condos, workplace locations, health care/medical facilities, schools and universities, airports, auto dealers, hotels, mixed-use municipal locations, parks and recreation areas, religious institutions, restaurants, retailers, stadiums, supermarkets, and transportation hubs.

For more information, please visit https://blinkcharging.com/

Forward-Looking Statements 

This press release contains forward-looking statements as defined within Section 27A of the Securities Act of 1933, as amended, and Section 21E of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended. These forward-looking statements, and terms such as “anticipate,” “expect,” “intend,” “may,” “will,” “should” or other comparable terms, involve risks and uncertainties because they relate to events and depend on circumstances that will occur in the future. Those statements include statements regarding the intent, belief, or current expectations of Blink Charging and members of its management, as well as the assumptions on which such statements are based. Prospective investors are cautioned that any such forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future performance and involve risks and uncertainties, including achieving projected revenue, adjusted EBITDA and gross margin targets as described in Blink Charging’s periodic reports filed with the SEC, and that actual results may differ materially from those contemplated by such forward-looking statements. Except as required by federal securities law, Blink Charging undertakes no obligation to update or revise forward-looking statements to reflect changed conditions.

PR@BlinkCharging.com

data.title: ASML Earnings Are Coming. What to Watch for in the AI and Memory Chip Boom. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Clark

ASML Holding is about to report its earnings. The chipmaking-equipment company's figures should give insight into confidence in the artificial-intelligence and memory processor cycles.

Dutch company ASML will report its earnings before the U.S. market open on Wednesday. It is expected to report a net quarterly profit of EUR2.55 billion ($3.00 billion) on revenue of EUR8.63 billion, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

ASML holds a near monopoly on lithography machines, which are crucial for manufacturing the most advanced semiconductors.

The large price tag of ASML's machines means revenue numbers can swing significantly from quarter to quarter, so investors tend to focus on its longer-term order numbers. However, ASML has said it will no longer disclose quarterly order numbers.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing, the world's most important chip manufacturer, recently said it would make enormous investments in production capacity this year and for several years ahead, which has buoyed investor sentiment toward ASML. Memory-chip companies such as South Korea's Samsung Electronics are also investing heavily in manufacturing.

One factor worth watching is how much of ASML's sales it expects to come from China. A bipartisan group of U.S. representatives recently introduced a bill to further restrict semiconductor manufacturing equipment sales to China, which could affect ASML's sales if it is passed into law and agreed with Dutch authorities.

During ASML's fourth-quarter earnings call in January, the company said it expects Chinese sales to comprise only 20% of 2026 sales. However, it made a similar prediction for 2025 and ultimately derived about a third of its sales from China last year.

ASML's American depositary receipts were up 0.6% in morning trading Tuesday, and have more than doubled over the past 12 months.

Write to Adam Clark at adam.clark@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Eco-building products maker STEICO's Q1 revenue falls on construction activity delay
data.description:
Overview

  • Germany wood fibre insulation maker's Q1 revenue fell 6.9% yr/yr on delayed construction activity

  • Q1 EBITDA declined due to higher energy and transport costs, lower hedging income

  • Company expects full-yr 2026 revenue to range from -2% to +4% yr/yr

Outlook

  • STEICO expects 2026 revenue between €375 mln and €398 mln

  • Company forecasts 2026 EBIT of €30 mln to €38 mln

Result Drivers

  • DELAYED CONSTRUCTION - Harsh winter weather in Europe delayed construction activity, reducing demand for STEICO products

  • COST INCREASES - Higher energy consumption from low temperatures and rising transport costs due to Middle East disruptions weighed on results

  • LOWER HEDGING AND CO2 INCOME - Reduced income from currency hedging and sale of CO2 emission allowances

data.title: Treasuries Move Notably Higher Following Inflation Data
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - After showing a lack of direction early in the session, treasuries moved notably higher over the course of the trading day on Tuesday.

Bond prices climbed firmly into positive territory after lingering near the unchanged in early trading. Subsequently, the yield on the benchmark ten-year note, which moves opposite of its price, slid 4.1 basis points to 4.256 percent.

The ten-year yield added to the 1.8 basis point dip seen on Monday, dropping to its lowest closing level in almost a month.

The strength among treasuries came after the Labor Department released a report showing tamer-than-expected producer price inflation in the month of March.

The Labor Department said its producer price index for final demand climbed by 0.5 percent in March, matching a downwardly revised increase in February.

Economists had expected producer prices to jump by 1.2 percent compared to the 0.7 percent advance originally reported for the previous month.

The report also said the annual rate of growth by producer prices accelerated to 4.0 percent in March from 3.4 percent in February, while economists had expected the pace of growth to surge to 4.6 percent.

'PPI rose at a much slower than expected rate in March, offering Fed officials some comfort the conflict won't spark a sustained inflation shock that could derail their rate cutting hopes,' said Nationwide Market Economist Oren Klachkin.

He added, 'Looking ahead, we're anticipating a hot PPI report for April, but do not see this evolving into a sustained inflation shock.'

Treasuries also benefitted from a sharp pullback by the price of crude oil, which fell sharply amid optimism about a second round of talks between the U.S. and Iran over ending the Middle East conflict.

President Donald Trump said on Monday the U.S. has been contacted by Iran about resuming peace talks and claimed, 'They'd like to make a deal very badly.'

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

data.title: Kering Q1 Revenue Down 6%
data.description:
PARIS (dpa-AFX) - Kering SA (PPRUF), the luxury goods company, on Tuesday reported first-quarter 2026 revenue of 3.57 billion euros, representing a 6 percent decline as reported, but remaining stable on a comparable basis.

The group's Fashion & Leather Goods segment experienced a revenue decline to 2.85 billion euros from 3.13 billion euros in the prior year. Gucci revenue decreased to 1.35 billion euros from 1.57 billion euros.

In contrast, the Jewelry segment delivered growth, rising to 269 million euros from 236 million euros, while Eyewear revenue increased to 489 million euros from 476 million euros.

PPRUF is currently trading at $329.00 up $17.83 or 5.73 percent on the OTC Markets.

data.title: ADT to Pay More Than $1.3M to Resolve Allegations of Illegally Charging Servicemembers
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

ADT will pay more than $1.3 million to resolve allegations that the home security services company unlawfully charged thousands of servicemembers for cancelling their ADT contracts after they received military relocation orders.

The Justice Department on Tuesday said ADT had imposed 30-day notice requirements on servicemembers who terminated their contracts with the company. Those requirements violated the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act, which allows servicemembers to end some consumer contracts after receiving a relocation order, the DOJ said.

The company will pay $1.26 million to more than 3,400 affected servicemembers, as well as $79,380 in civil penalties, the DOJ said. ADT will also make changes to its policies and training to avoid future violations of the SCRA.

ADT didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

"The law is clear — when servicemembers receive relocation orders, they have the right to terminate contracts without penalty beyond the current billing period," U.S. Attorney Jason Reding Quiñones said. "This settlement returns over $1.3 million to affected servicemembers and ensures that companies understand those protections are not optional."

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: The Art Market Returns to Life. Impressionism Pops. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Abby Schultz

Investment returns in the art market are turning a corner after the most dismal period for collectors since the art market crashed in the early 1990s.

The mean compound annual return of art sold worldwide in the first quarter by Christie's, Phillips, and Sotheby's — the top auction houses — was 1.7%, up from -0.01% in the fourth quarter and 0% a year ago, according to academics Jianping Mei and Michael Moses, co-founders of ArtistIP.com.

Mei and Moses study repeat sales of artworks sold at public auctions to calculate returns. Their results are in a new paper titled "Some Signs of Life."

That profits are improving may not be a surprise given that total sales at the major auction houses rose a robust 64.3% in the first quarter from a year earlier to $1.7 billion, including the buyer's premium, according to ArtTactic, a London data and analytics firm. That was the strongest first quarter recorded since 2016, ArtTactic said.

But revenue and profit are two different things. "You could be increasing revenue, but losing money on every work you sell — only looking at revenue can be misleading," Moses says.

To calculate returns, Mei and Moses study art bought at auctions since 1970 and sold since 2000, looking at works that were resold multiple times. In the first quarter, they found 1,136 paintings with a repeat sale pair history, and another 202 paintings with a repeat sale history that didn't sell.

The 1.7% uptick in the mean compound annual return is good news, but Mei and Moses found 47% of sales in their repeat sale universe still posted a negative return for the quarter, and about 25% didn't sell. A group of 36 British paintings with repeat sales posted the best mean return of 3.4% in the first quarter, followed by a larger group of 412 impressionist works with a mean return of 3.1%. Post war and contemporary resales of 329 works eked out only a 0.3% mean return.

Mei and Moses' research also largely debunked two often-stated truisms of the art world: That works bought for high prices outperform peers and that works owned for longer periods perform best.

On the price question, Mei and Moses found that lower-priced works could fetch high prices, but results are very unpredictable. Returns for works priced below $1 million ranged from a nearly negative 80% to plus 200%. For works priced above $1 million, however, the return window was smaller, ranging from negative 30% to plus 15%.

The highest price for a resale pair tracked in the first quarter was Wassily Kandinsky's Le Rond Rouge, 1939 — which sold for $20.6 million at Sotheby's in 2018, and then again on March 5 at Christie's in London for GBP12.5 million ($16.8 million), a negative return of 2.7%.

Mei and Moses also discovered that the longer a collector owned a painting, the less profit he or she would generally make. A painting sold less than five years after it was bought returned 7% on average, but only 3.5% if sold within 10 years, and 1.4% if sold within 20. The calculation changes when a painting is owned for 50 years or more. Then the average return is 4.9%, with a low risk per unit of return on par with 90-day Treasuries.

When art is held for multiple generations, "the better you are going to do on a risk-adjusted basis," Moses says.

One way to create more certainty of future returns is to buy a piece of art for a price below the auction house's estimate, which is roughly its mark-to-market value.

A good example of a stellar short-term return earned by a seller, and the risks for a new buyer was evident in Sotheby's January New York sale of Martin Johnson Heade's Sun Gems, 1863-65, which sold for nearly $700,000, far above a high estimate of $180,000. The anonymous seller fetched an unexpected 25% return as he had bought the work for $210,000 in 2018.

"Clearly there were two people in the house who really liked that painting," Moses says. But he cautions that paying a high multiple above the estimate can limit future performance. "Some of these returns are achieved by good buying and being careful not to get into a bidding war, " Moses says.

Write to Abby Schultz at abby.schultz@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Kevin Warsh's Fed Chair Hearing Is Set for Next Tuesday — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Nicole Goodkind

The Senate Banking Committee will hold a confirmation hearing next Tuesday at 10 a.m. Eastern for Kevin Warsh, President Donald Trump's nominee to lead the Federal Reserve.

The hearing is a critical step toward installing new leadership at the Fed. Warsh would replace Fed Chair Jerome Powell, also nominated by Trump, whose term expires May 15.

"The Committee On Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs will meet in open session, hybrid format to conduct a hearing on the nomination of The Honorable Kevin Warsh, to be a Member and Chairman of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System," the committee announced on Tuesday afternoon.

Committee Chair Tim Scott said on Fox Business earlier that he plans to "talk through the economy." He added that "we'll talk through price stability and inflation. We'll talk about the independence of the Fed."

Warsh's paperwork, including his financial disclosures and committee questionnaire, has been submitted to the banking panel, clearing a large procedural hurdle.

Warsh, a former Fed governor who teaches at Stanford's Hoover Institution and works in finance, would take over at a difficult moment.

Inflation is running near 3%, well above the Fed's 2% target. The labor market is showing signs of strain. Oil prices have climbed amid the war in the Middle East, and the ripple effects of tariffs on consumer prices remain hard to predict. Against that backdrop, the Fed has held interest rates steady at its last two meetings, and officials have signaled little appetite to cut.

That puts Warsh in a delicate spot from the start. Trump has called for lower interest rates, and Warsh has outlined a plan to shrink the Fed's balance sheet while also cutting rates, a more aggressive approach than the current Fed has been willing to take. Senators from both parties are likely to press him on how he squares that vision with an economy where inflation hasn't yet been fully tamed.

But getting through the hearing is only half the battle. Even if Warsh performs well before the committee, there is no clear path to a confirmation vote on the Senate floor.

Sen. Thom Tillis, a North Carolina Republican who sits on the banking panel, has vowed to block Warsh's nomination from advancing until the Justice Department drops its criminal investigation into Powell. Because Republicans hold only a one-vote majority on the committee, Tillis alone can prevent Warsh from being sent to the full Senate. He has shown no sign of budging.

The investigation centers on cost overruns tied to a renovation of the Fed's Washington headquarters and Powell's testimony to Congress about the project. A federal judge has twice blocked the probe, rejecting subpoenas issued by U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro and then refusing to reconsider that decision. Pirro has said she would appeal, a process that could take months or longer.

Scott said Tuesday he believes the Justice Department will wrap up the investigation within weeks, which he said would free Tillis to vote yes on Warsh. But Scott also acknowledged he had no specific knowledge of the DOJ's plans.

Powell, for his part, has said he intends to stay on at the Fed until the investigation is resolved. His term as chair expires May 15, but he holds a separate seat on the Fed's Board of Governors that runs through January 2028, and he has no obligation to give it up.

If Warsh isn't confirmed by mid-May, Powell has said he would remain as acting chair in the interim, meaning the man Trump is trying to replace could still be setting interest rate policy well into the summer.

Barron's will bring you live coverage of the hearing next week.

Write to Nicole Goodkind at nicole.goodkind@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Argentina inflation ticks up in March
data.description:
Argentina's monthly inflation rate was 3.4% in March, up from the 2.9% registered in February and above analysts' forecast of 3.0%, official data showed on Tuesday.

In the 12 months through March prices rose 32.6%, edging down the previous month's rate of 33.1% but surpassing analysts' 32.1% forecast.

Education costs led the monthly increase, jumping 12.1% at the start of the school year. Transport prices rose 4.1%, driven by higher costs for fuel, public transport, and airfare.

President Javier Milei added in a post on social media that he expects inflation to return to a downward trend in the future. In a separate post, Economy Minister Luis Caputo said in the monthly figure saw a significant impact from the war in the Middle East, following trends in other countries worldwide.

On Monday, a Reuters poll showed analysts expect Argentina's consumer price inflation to cool this year by less than previously expected, ending 2026 at its lowest rate in nine years at 30%, even as economic growth expectations held steady.

A central bank poll released last week had similar forecasts, with analysts predicting inflation will end this year at 29.1% - 3.1 percentage points above prior estimates, while slightly edging down economic growth forecasts to 3.3%.

data.title: Canada Finance Minister to Present Spring Economic Update on April 28 — Update
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canada intends to present an economic update on April 28 that will shed light on the impact of higher energy prices on the government's bottom line.

Finance Minister François-Philippe Champagne said Tuesday the update would reinforce government efforts to rebuild an economy that became too reliant on trade with the U.S. to spark growth.

Canada's Liberal government changed last year the timing of the government's budgeting process. Under the new system, officials now issue an annual budget plan in the fall, to be followed by an update in the spring. Previously, it was the reverse, with an update in the fall and the budget in the spring.

Earlier Tuesday, Canada said it would provide a temporary fuel-tax cut--starting next week until Labor Day-- to help households cope with the financial strain from higher energy prices. Gas prices have climbed more than 30% due to conflict in the Middle East, pitting the U.S. versus Iran, which has largely stalled oil-tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz.

Canada is a net exporter of crude oil, so national income stands to increase from higher energy prices. Economists at BMO Capital Markets estimate a 40% rise in oil prices for a full year could boost government revenue by nearly 5 billion Canadian dollars, or the equivalent of US$3.62 billion.

BMO economists expect the overall impact on federal finances from higher energy prices this time to be neutral, as higher revenue would be offset by slower economic activity and a rise in borrowing costs. Their estimate was provided prior to the Liberal government's plan for fuel-tax relief.

The Bank of Canada, in its most recent economic outlook, anticipated 1.1% growth this year. As it happens, the central bank issues its next rate-policy decision the day after the spring economic update, on April 29.

The International Monetary Fund on Tuesday said it expects Canada to record 1.5% growth in 2026.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: U.S. Natural Gas Futures Extend Losing Streak — Market Talk
data.description: U.S. natural gas futures slip for a fifth consecutive session with a mild weather outlook pointing to sluggish demand and strong inventory builds. A drop in benchmark European gas prices along with oil futures curbed what little of the Middle East risk premium has seeped into the U.S. natural gas market. "What's also aiding weakness in U.S. natural gas markets are bearish April weather patterns and relatively healthy supplies as [storage] surpluses increase towards +150 Bcf after the next two EIA reports are accounted for," NatGasWeather.com says in a note. Nymex natural gas settles down 1.1% at $2.599/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)
